title: ἡγεμονεύω
content:
ἡγεμονεύω

ἡγεμονεύω (G2230)

verb | ἄγω

37.60 · Control, Rule · Rule, Govern

to be a governor (derivative of ἡγεμών[a] ‘ruler,’ 37.59) to be a governor, in the NT restricted to the meaning of a governor of a Roman province The expression ‘Quirinius was governor of Syria’ (LUK 2:2) may be rendered in some languages as ‘Quirinius was the one who commanded all those in the province of Syria’ or ‘Quirinius gave orders to all those in Syria.’

LUK 2:2 · LUK 3:1

title: ἡγεμονία
content:
ἡγεμονία

ἡγεμονία (G2231)

noun, f. | -ας | ἄγω

37.58 · Control, Rule · Rule, Govern

government, rule ruling over, with the implication of providing direction and leadership In a number of languages it may be necessary to express the concept of ‘ruling’ by some form of ‘command,’ for example, ‘he commands people what they must do’ or ‘he orders people’ or ‘he is the chief over people.’

LUK 3:1

title: ἡγεμών
content:
ἡγεμών

ἡγεμών (G2232)

noun, m. | -όνος | ἄγω

37.59 · Control, Rule · Rule, Govern

ruler (derivative of ἡγέομαι[c] ‘to rule,’ 37.58) one who rules, with the implication of preeminent position In the context of MAT 2:6 it is possible to understand ‘rulers’ as a figurative reference to important places, and therefore one may also translate ‘you are by no means the least among the leading cities of Judah.’

MAT 2:6 · MAT 10:18 · MRK 13:9 · LUK 21:12

37.83 · Control, Rule · Rule, Govern

prefect, governor (the Greek equivalent of the Latin term praefectus) a person who ruled over a minor Roman province

MAT 27:2 · MAT 27:11 · MAT 27:11 · MAT 27:14 · MAT 27:15 · MAT 27:21 · MAT 27:27 · MAT 28:14 · LUK 20:20 · ACT 23:24 · ACT 23:26 · ACT 23:33 · ACT 24:1 · ACT 24:10 · ACT 26:30 · 1PE 2:14

title: ἡγέομαι
content:
ἡγέομαι

ἡγέομαι (G2233)

verb | ἄγω

31.1 · Hold a View, Believe, Trust · Have an Opinion, Hold a View

to hold a view, to have an opinion, to consider, to regard to hold a view or have an opinion with regard to something

ACT 26:2 · 2CO 9:5 · PHP 2:3 · PHP 2:6 · PHP 2:25 · PHP 3:7 · PHP 3:8 · PHP 3:8 · 1TH 5:13 · 2TH 3:15 · 1TI 1:12 · 1TI 6:1 · HEB 10:29 · HEB 11:11 · HEB 11:26 · JAS 1:2 · 2PE 1:13 · 2PE 2:13 · 2PE 3:9 · 2PE 3:15

36.1 · Guide, Discipline, Follow · Guide, Lead

to guide, to direct, to lead to so influence others as to cause them to follow a recommended course of action In some languages it is difficult to distinguish readily between expressions for ‘leading’ and those which refer to ‘ruling’ or ‘governing,’ but it is important to try to distinguish clearly between these two different sets of interpersonal relations. In some languages, the concept of ‘leading’ can be expressed by ‘showing how to’ or ‘demonstrating how one ought to.’ In other languages it is possible to speak of ‘leading’ as simply ‘going ahead of,’ but too often such an expression may designate only ‘a scout’ who goes ahead to see whether things are safe, or it may refer only to a person who insists on his prerogative as the most distinguished person in a group.

LUK 22:26 · ACT 14:12 · ACT 15:22 · HEB 13:7 · HEB 13:171 · HEB 13:24

37.58 · Control, Rule · Rule, Govern

to rule over, to order, to govern to rule over, with the implication of providing direction and leadership In a number of languages it may be necessary to express the concept of ‘ruling’ by some form of ‘command,’ for example, ‘he commands people what they must do’ or ‘he orders people’ or ‘he is the chief over people.’

MAT 2:6 · ACT 7:10 · ACT 15:22 · HEB 13:171 · HEB 13:24

1 HEB 13:17 Though the basic denotation of ἡγούμενος is ‘to lead,’ semantic features of ruling and high status are also connotatively involved.

title: ἡδέως
content:
ἡδέως

ἡδέως (G2234)

advb.

25.129 · Attitudes and Emotions · Happy, Glad, Joyful

gladly, happily pertaining to experiencing happiness, based primarily upon the pleasure derived

MRK 6:20 · MRK 12:37 · 2CO 11:19 · 2CO 12:9 · 2CO 12:15

title: ἤδη
content:
ἤδη

ἤδη (G2235)

advb.

67.20 · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Other Points of Time: Before, Long Ago, Now, At the Same Time, When, About, After

already a point of time preceding another point of time and implying completion Note that the subsequent point of time in LUK 11:7 and MAT 3:10 is the act of speaking.

MAT 3:10 · MAT 5:28 · MAT 14:15 · MAT 14:24 · MAT 15:32 · MAT 17:12 · MAT 24:32 · MRK 4:37 · MRK 6:35 · MRK 6:35 · MRK 8:2 · MRK 11:11 · MRK 13:28 · MRK 15:42 · MRK 15:44 · LUK 3:9 · LUK 7:6 · LUK 11:7 · LUK 12:49 · LUK 14:17 · LUK 19:37 · LUK 21:30 · LUK 21:30 · LUK 23:44 · LUK 24:29 · JHN 3:18 · JHN 4:35 · JHN 4:51 · JHN 5:6 · JHN 6:17 · JHN 7:14 · JHN 9:22 · JHN 9:27 · JHN 11:17 · JHN 11:39 · JHN 13:2 · JHN 15:3 · JHN 19:28 · JHN 19:33 · JHN 21:4 · JHN 21:14 · ACT 4:3 · ACT 27:9 · ACT 27:9 · ROM 13:11 · 1CO 4:8 · 1CO 4:8 · 1CO 5:3 · 1CO 6:7 · PHP 3:12 · PHP 3:12 · 2TH 2:7 · 1TI 5:15 · 2TI 2:18 · 2TI 4:6 · 1JN 2:8 · 1JN 4:3

67.40 · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Other Points of Time: Before, Long Ago, Now, At the Same Time, When, About, After

Collocation: ἤδη ποτέ

already now, now at last, now at length (an idiom, literally: already sometime) a time simultaneous with the discourse but in relationship to preceding time

ROM 1:10 · PHP 4:10 · 2PE 3:1

title: ἡδονή
content:
ἡδονή

ἡδονή (G2237)

noun, f. | -ῆς | ἥδομαι (enjoy oneself) · αὐθάδης · ἡδύοσμον · συνήδομαι · φιλήδονος

25.111 · Attitudes and Emotions · Enjoy, Take Pleasure In, Be Fond of Doing

that which is pleasurable, pleasure that which someone is fond of doing, in that it produces enjoyment

LUK 8:14 · JAS 4:3 · 2PE 2:13

25.27 · Attitudes and Emotions · Desire Strongly

desire, passion, desire for pleasure desire for physical pleasure, often sexual

TIT 3:3 · JAS 4:1

title: ἡδύοσμον
content:
ἡδύοσμον

ἡδύοσμον (G2238)

noun, n. | -ου | ἡδύς + ὄζω

3.23 · Plants · Plants That Are Not Trees

mint fragrant plant (genus Mentha); up to 2 m high; small, soft leaves with sawlike margins; used in cooking, tea, medicine In designating ‘mint’ as well as ἄνηθον ‘dill,’ πήγανον ‘rue,’ and κύμινον ‘cumin,’ it is probably wise to borrow a term and use a classifier, for example, ‘a seasoning herb called mint.’

MAT 23:23 · LUK 11:42

title: ἦθος
content:
ἦθος

ἦθος (G2239)

noun, n. | -ους | ἐθίζω

41.25 · Behavior and Related States · Custom, Tradition

custom, habit a pattern of behavior more or less fixed by tradition and generally sanctioned by the society In a number of languages there is no noun for ‘custom’ or ‘habit,’ but the same meaning is communicated by verbal aspects or adverbial phrases indicating habitual action. For example, in the case of JHN 19:40 one may translate in some languages ‘as is the way in which Jews always bury people,’ and for 1CO 11:16 one may translate ‘this is not what we constantly do.’

1CO 15:33

title: ἥκω
content:
ἥκω

ἥκω (G2240)

verb | pres.3pl. ἥκασιν | ἀνήκει · καθήκει

15.84 · Linear Movement · Come, Come To, Arrive

to come to, to reach, to arrive to move toward and to arrive at a point1

MAT 8:11 · LUK 13:29 · JHN 2:4 · JHN 4:47 · JHN 6:37 · JHN 8:422 · ROM 11:26 · HEB 10:7 · HEB 10:9 · HEB 10:37 · REV 2:25 · REV 3:3 · REV 3:3 · REV 3:9 · REV 15:4

85.10 · Existence in Space · Be in a Place

to be here, to be there to be in a place, as the result of having arrived For another interpretation of ἥκω in JHN 8:42, see 15.84.

MAT 24:50 · MRK 8:3 · LUK 12:46 · LUK 15:27 · JHN 8:422 · HEB 10:7 · HEB 10:9 · HEB 10:37 · 1JN 5:20

13.112 · Be, Become, Exist, Happen · Happen

to happen, to have happened to have come or to be present, with respect to some temporal reference point

MAT 23:36 · MAT 24:14 · LUK 13:35 · LUK 19:43 · 2PE 3:10 · REV 18:8

1 There are no doubt certain subtle distinctions of meaning in ἐφικνέομαιφθάνω[a], καταντάω[a], and ἥκω[a], but it is not possible to determine what the distinctive features are on the basis of existing contexts. 2 JHN 8:42 In a context such as JHN 8:42, it is possible to understand ἥκω in the sense of being in a particular location rather than having moved to such a point (see 85.10).

title: ἠλί
content:
ἠλί

ἠλί (G2241) — ηλι

noun (Aramaic loanword) | indeclinable

12.3 · Supernatural Beings and Powers · Supernatural Beings

my God a Hebrew term meaning my God For certain problems involved in rendering an expression such as ‘my God,’ see the discussion in 12.1.

MAT 27:46 · MAT 27:46

title: Ἠλί
content:
Ἠλί

Ἠλί (G2242)

noun-name, m. | indeclinable

93.141 · Names of Persons and Places · Persons

Heli a person in the genealogy of Jesus

LUK 3:23

title: Ἠλίας
content:
Ἠλίας

Ἠλίας (G2243)

noun-name, m. | -ου

93.142 · Names of Persons and Places · Persons

Elijah a major OT prophet

MAT 11:14 · MAT 16:14 · MAT 17:3 · MAT 17:4 · MAT 17:10 · MAT 17:11 · MAT 17:12 · MAT 27:47 · MAT 27:49 · MRK 6:15 · MRK 8:28 · MRK 9:4 · MRK 9:5 · MRK 9:11 · MRK 9:12 · MRK 9:13 · MRK 15:35 · MRK 15:36 · LUK 1:17 · LUK 4:25 · LUK 4:26 · LUK 9:8 · LUK 9:19 · LUK 9:30 · LUK 9:33 · JHN 1:21 · JHN 1:25 · ROM 11:2 · JAS 5:17

title: ἡλικία
content:
ἡλικία

ἡλικία (G2244)

noun, f. | -ας | ἡλίκος

67.151 · Time · Indefinite Units of Time: Age, Lifetime, Interval, Period

span of life, lifetime, age the period of time when a person is alive In MAT 6:27 ἡλικία may also denote height (see 81.4). If one understands ἡλικία in MAT 6:27 as time, then obviously some adjustment needs to be made to the meaning of πῆχυς ‘cubit’ (81.25). For many languages the most appropriate term is one which means ‘day,’ but it would also be possible to use some other unit of length if this would clearly imply an impossibility.

MAT 6:27 · LUK 12:25

67.156 · Time · Indefinite Units of Time: Age, Lifetime, Interval, Period

prime, of age, mature the period of life when one is mature and at one’s prime1 Normally the phrase παρὰ καιρὸν ἡλικίας (HEB 11:11) would refer to a woman beyond menopause.

JHN 9:21 · JHN 9:23 · EPH 4:13 · HEB 11:11

81.4 · Spacial Dimensions · High, Low, Deep

height, stature height as the dimension of stature of an animate object

MAT 6:27 · LUK 2:52 · LUK 12:25 · LUK 19:3

1 The meanings of terms in 67.152-67.158 could almost be treated equally well in Domain 23 Physiological Processes and States, since these could be regarded as simply derivatives of physiological states. However, the focus of meaning in the respective contexts seems to be more upon a particular period of time rather than upon a physiological state or condition.

title: ἡλίκος
content:
ἡλίκος

ἡλίκος (G2245)

pirg. | -η, -ον | ἡλικία · συνηλικιώτης

79.127 · Features of Objects · Large, Small

how extensive, how large, how small an interrogative of size occurring in indirect questions

JAS 3:5 · JAS 3:5

78.13 · Degree · Much, Little [Positive-Negative Degree]

how, very, how great, how much, intense, severe a relatively high point on a scale involving exclamation In a number of languages an expression of high degree in an exclamation can only be reproduced as an expression of intensive degree in a non-exclamatory form. For example, the statement in MAT 6:23 ‘how great must the darkness be!’ must be rendered in a number of languages as ‘the darkness is very, very intense’ or ‘it is very, very dark there.’
In a number of languages the equivalent of ἡλίκον ἀγῶνα ἔχω in COL 2:1 would be ‘I struggle very much indeed.’

COL 2:1

title: ἥλιος
content:
ἥλιος

ἥλιος (G2246)

noun, m. | -ου | εἰλικρίνεια

1.28 · Geographical Objects and Features · Heavenly Bodies

the sun the large celestial body that gives warmth and light to earth in the daytime Translators have relatively few difficulties in obtaining a satisfactory term for the sun, though there may be a tendency in some languages to add some kind of honorific title to the word, for example, ‘our father the sun.’ Translators’ problems occur, however, with verbs describing the action or effect of the sun. For example, in MAT 13:6, instead of speaking of ‘the sun rising,’ it may be more satisfactory to speak of ‘the sun shining’ or, in this particular context, possibly ‘when the sun was overhead’ or ‘by the time the sun had reached the sky.’ In MRK 13:24 one can perhaps best render the meaning as ‘the sun will no longer shine’ or ‘... will cease shining.’

MAT 5:45 · MAT 13:6 · MAT 13:43 · MAT 17:2 · MAT 24:29 · MRK 1:32 · MRK 4:6 · MRK 13:24 · MRK 16:2 · LUK 4:40 · LUK 21:25 · LUK 23:45 · ACT 2:20 · ACT 13:11 · ACT 26:13 · ACT 27:20 · 1CO 15:41 · EPH 4:26 · JAS 1:11 · REV 1:16 · REV 6:12 · REV 7:2 · REV 7:16 · REV 8:12 · REV 9:2 · REV 10:1 · REV 12:1 · REV 16:8 · REV 16:12 · REV 19:17 · REV 21:23 · REV 22:5

title: ἧλος
content:
ἧλος

ἧλος (G2247)

noun, m. | -ου | προσηλόω

6.22 · Artifacts · Instruments Used in Binding and Fastening

nail, spike a thin piece of metal (usually iron) sharpened on one end (in the NT used only in connection with nails employed in the crucifixion) In a number of languages a distinction is made between relatively small nails and larger spikes; the latter would be appropriate in speaking of crucifixion.

JHN 20:25 · JHN 20:25

title: ἡμέρα
content:
ἡμέρα

ἡμέρα (G2250)

noun, f. | -ας | ἐφημερία · ἐφήμερος · καθημερινός · μεσημβρία · νυχθήμερον · ὀκταήμερος · σήμερον

67.178 · Time · Definite Units of Time: Year, Month, Week, Day, Hour

day according to Hebrew reckoning (as reflected in the NT), a period of time beginning at sunset and ending at the following sunset

MAT 26:2 · MRK 14:1 · MRK 14:12 · LUK 1:59 · LUK 2:21 · LUK 2:43 · LUK 4:16 · LUK 13:14 · LUK 13:14 · LUK 13:16 · LUK 14:5 · LUK 22:7 · LUK 23:54 · JHN 5:9 · JHN 7:37 · JHN 9:14 · JHN 12:1 · JHN 19:31 · JHN 20:19 · ACT 2:1 · ACT 7:8 · ACT 12:3 · ACT 13:14 · ACT 16:13 · ACT 20:6 · ACT 20:16 · ACT 21:26 · ACT 21:26 · ACT 21:27 · ROM 14:6 · GAL 4:10 · HEB 4:4 · REV 1:10

day the daylight period between sunrise and sunset (divided into twelve hours)

67.142 · Time · Indefinite Units of Time: Age, Lifetime, Interval, Period

time, period an indefinite unit of time (whether grammatically singular or plural), but not particularly long (note the contrast with αἰών[a] ‘age,’ 67.143, and γενεά[d] ‘age,’ 67.144) In a number of languages there is no specific term such as ‘period’ referring to an indefinite extent of time. One can, however, render LUK 1:23 as ‘when he no longer had to serve in the Temple’ or ‘when he had finished the days when he served in the Temple.’

MAT 2:1 · MAT 3:1 · MAT 9:15 · MAT 11:12 · MAT 17:23 · MAT 23:30 · MAT 24:19 · MAT 24:22 · MAT 24:22 · MAT 24:29 · MAT 24:37 · MAT 24:38 · MAT 28:15 · MAT 28:20 · MRK 1:9 · MRK 2:1 · MRK 2:20 · MRK 2:20 · MRK 8:1 · MRK 13:17 · MRK 13:19 · MRK 13:20 · MRK 13:20 · MRK 13:24 · LUK 1:5 · LUK 1:20 · LUK 1:23 · LUK 1:24 · LUK 1:25 · LUK 1:39 · LUK 1:75 · LUK 1:80 · LUK 2:1 · LUK 2:6 · LUK 2:22 · LUK 4:2 · LUK 4:25 · LUK 5:35 · LUK 5:35 · LUK 6:12 · LUK 6:23 · LUK 9:36 · LUK 9:51 · LUK 17:22 · LUK 17:26 · LUK 17:26 · LUK 17:28 · LUK 19:43 · LUK 21:6 · LUK 21:22 · LUK 21:23 · LUK 23:7 · LUK 23:29 · LUK 24:18 · JHN 8:56 · JHN 16:26 · ACT 2:17 · ACT 2:18 · ACT 2:29 · ACT 3:24 · ACT 5:36 · ACT 5:37 · ACT 6:1 · ACT 7:41 · ACT 7:45 · ACT 9:37 · ACT 11:27 · ACT 13:41 · ACT 15:7 · ACT 15:36 · ACT 16:12 · ACT 16:18 · ACT 18:18 · ACT 21:5 · ACT 21:10 · ACT 21:15 · ACT 21:38 · ACT 24:24 · ACT 25:13 · ACT 25:14 · ACT 27:7 · ACT 27:20 · ROM 8:36 · 2CO 6:2 · 2CO 6:2 · EPH 5:16 · EPH 6:13 · 2TI 3:1 · HEB 1:2 · HEB 3:8 · HEB 5:7 · HEB 7:3 · HEB 8:8 · HEB 8:9 · HEB 8:10 · HEB 10:16 · HEB 10:32 · HEB 12:10 · JAS 5:3 · 1PE 3:10 · 1PE 3:20 · 2PE 3:3 · 2PE 3:7 · 2PE 3:18 · REV 2:13 · REV 6:17 · REV 9:6 · REV 10:7 · REV 11:6

14.40 · Physical Events and States · Light

daylight the light of the day in contrast with the darkness of night (see νύξ[b] ‘night,’ 14.59)1 In order to focus attention upon the beginning of daylight, one may translate LUK 4:42 as ‘when the sun began to shine’ or ‘when the sky began to become light’ or even ‘when it was no longer night.’
In some languages there is no noun for either ‘day’ or ‘daylight,’ but a verb expression may be employed which may be rendered literally as ‘to day’ or ‘to become day.’

LUK 4:42 · LUK 6:13 · JHN 11:9 · ACT 12:18 · ACT 16:35 · ACT 23:12 · ACT 27:29 · ACT 27:33 · ACT 27:39 · ROM 13:13 · 1TH 5:5 · 1TH 5:8 · 2PE 2:13

56.1 · Courts and Legal Procedures · Court of Justice

court, court of justice a court of justice for determining guilt or innocence2 For languages which do not have technical terms for a court, it is usually possible to use some such phrase as ‘to be brought before a judge’ or ‘to have judges decide one’s case.’

1CO 4:3

67.92 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

Collocation: ἡμέρα ἐξ ἡμέρας · καθ᾽ ἡμέραν

for a long time, for quite a while, day after day, every day (idioms, literally: day out of day, and: throughout day, respectively) a relatively long period of time

MAT 26:55 · MRK 14:49 · LUK 9:23 · LUK 11:3 · LUK 16:19 · LUK 19:47 · LUK 22:53 · ACT 2:46 · ACT 2:47 · ACT 3:2 · ACT 16:5 · ACT 17:11 · ACT 19:9 · 1CO 15:31 · 2CO 11:28 · HEB 3:13 · HEB 7:27 · HEB 10:11 · 2PE 2:8 · 2PE 2:8

56.33 · Courts and Legal Procedures · Judge, Condemn, Acquit

Collocation: ἡμέρα σφαγῆς

day of condemnation (an idiom, literally: day of slaughter) a time of destructive judgment The reference of the phrase ἡμέρᾳ σφαγῆς in JAS 5:5 is the destructive judgment of God, but it is possible that σφαγῆς should be interpreted in a more literal sense of ‘destruction’ rather than mere condemnation.

JAS 5:5

67.93 · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

Collocation: ἡμέρας τεσσεράκοντα καὶ νύκτας τεσσεράκοντα · ἡμέρας τεσσεράκοντα

a long time (idioms, literally: forty days and forty nights, forty days, respectively) possibly a symbolic temporal expression denoting a relatively long period of time Most translations render ἡμέρας τεσσεράκοντα καὶ νύκτας τεσσεράκοντα as either ‘forty days and forty nights’ or ‘forty days,’ though it is possible, in view of the symbolic meaning of the number ‘forty,’ that this expression is to be understood as meaning a long period of time.

MAT 4:2 · MRK 1:13 · LUK 4:2 · ACT 1:3

to be old (an idiom, literally: to advance in days) to be quite advanced in age

LUK 1:7 · LUK 1:18 · LUK 2:36

67.92a · Time · Duration of Time without Reference to Points or Units of Time: Time, Spend Time, Always, Eternal, Old, Immediately, Young

Collocation: νυκτὸς καὶ ἡμέρας · ἡμέρας καὶ νυκτός · ἡμέρᾳ καὶ ἡμέρᾳ

day and night, always, constantly, continually (expressions, literally: night and day, day and night, day and day, respectively) duration of time, either continuous or episodic, but without limits

67.59 · Time · A Point of Time with Reference to Other Points of Time: Before, Long Ago, Now, At the Same Time, When, About, After

Collocation: δι᾽ ἡμερῶν

a few days later (an idiom, literally: through days) a point of time subsequent to another point of time after an interval of a few days

MRK 2:1

1 ἡμέρα[d] daylight overlaps considerably in meaning with ἡμέρα[b] daylight period (67.178). In LUK 4:42, for example, one could interpret ἡμέρα as a period of time rather than as light associated with daytime. See also 14.59. 2 There are no doubt certain subtle differences of meaning in these terms, especially in their connotations, but it is not possible to specify the difference on the basis of existing contexts.

title: ἦχος
